title: Coreopsis involucrata
intro: Coreopsis involucrata, also known as Missouri tickseed, is a perennial herbaceous plant native to North America, belonging to the sunflower family (Asteraceae). Traditionally, Missouri tickseed has been used in herbal medicine for its antiseptic, anti-inflammatory, and antiviral properties, providing relief from various health issues such as wounds, fever, and respiratory infections. The bioactive constituents of Coreopsis involucrata include flavonoids, sesquiterpene lactones, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made from Missouri tickseed include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used to support immune function and alleviate symptoms associated with colds and flu.
taxonomy: Coreopsis involucrata is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Asteraceae. This plant belongs to the genus Coreopsis, which consists of about 80 species of plants. Coreopsis involucrata is native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico. It is a perennial plant that grows up to 1-2 feet tall and produces yellow or pink flowers. As a member of the Asteraceae family, Coreopsis involucrata is closely related to other daisy-like plants.
uses: Coreopsis involucrata has been traditionally used by various cultures for its medicinal properties. The plant's roots and leaves are believed to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, which may help soothe skin irritations and wounds. Some studies suggest that Coreopsis involucrata may have antimicrobial properties, potentially aiding in the treatment of infections. Additionally, the plant has been used to treat digestive issues, such as diarrhea and stomach cramps, due to its supposed carminative and astringent properties. More research is needed to fully understand the plant's medicinal uses and potential benefits.
